transcript.presenting_concern: Behavioral experiments for fear of public speaking at work

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Samantha, welcome back. How are you doing today?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Good, actually. Kind of nervous to tell you about the homework, but good.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Nervous to tell me because you're worried about my reaction, or nervous because of what happened?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [laughs] Both? No — actually it went well. I'm nervous because I don't want to jinx it by saying it went well. Like if I admit progress, the universe will punish me.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's a thought we should note — "if I acknowledge success, something bad will happen." That's a form of magical thinking. Let's bookmark it and come back. First, tell me about the behavioral experiment. Last session we discussed creating one around your fear of public speaking at work. What happened?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Okay, so I did it. I volunteered to present the Q2 metrics in our team meeting on Wednesday. It was a five-minute presentation to twelve people — my direct team plus my manager and two people from marketing. I thought I was going to throw up beforehand. Like physically felt nauseous at my desk.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: But you did it. You followed through despite the nausea and the fear. Before we debrief the content, I want to acknowledge that. The hardest part of a behavioral experiment is showing up. You showed up. Now walk me through the experience — before, during, and after.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Before: I prepared excessively. Like, I rehearsed it fifteen times. I scripted every single word — not just bullet points, the actual sentences I would say. I practiced in front of my bathroom mirror. I practiced with my cat as the audience. The morning of, my anxiety was probably a 9 out of 10. I considered calling in sick — that thought came at about 7 AM when my alarm went off. I had the excuse ready: "I think I'm coming down with something." But then I thought about how we talked about avoidance reinforcing the cycle and I made myself get dressed.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Let me pause there. You had a 9 out of 10 anxiety, a fully formed avoidance plan, and you still chose to go in. What tipped the scale?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Honestly? I didn't want to come in here next week and tell you I bailed. But also — underneath that — I was tired of being the person who never speaks up. I've watched people with half my competence get promoted because they present well. I'm tired of being invisible.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: So your values — career advancement, being seen for your competence — were ultimately stronger than the anxiety's pull toward avoidance. That's important. Values-driven action in the presence of fear. Continue — what happened during the presentation?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I was shaky for the first minute. Like, I could physically hear my voice quavering. My hands were trembling — I had notes on a piece of paper and the paper was visibly shaking. I was sure everyone could see it. I had this moment of "oh god, they all know I'm terrified, this is humiliating." But then — and I don't know exactly when it happened — maybe about ninety seconds in, I got into the data. The numbers themselves were interesting and I was explaining a trend I'd noticed and I kind of forgot to be anxious for a stretch. Maybe thirty seconds of flow.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Thirty seconds of flow. That's significant — that's evidence that absorption in content reduces self-focus. What happened after the flow moment?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I stumbled on a number — said "forty-two percent" when it was "forty-four percent" — and corrected myself. "Sorry, forty-four." My anxiety spiked back up to maybe a 6 or 7 for a moment because I thought "everyone noticed that mistake" but then I kept going and it settled back down. The whole thing took about six minutes. I wrapped up and said "any questions?" Nobody had any. And... that was it.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And after? What was the room like? What feedback did you get?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: My manager said "nice job" in the chat — we were on a hybrid meeting, some people on Zoom. Two colleagues messaged me afterward asking for the slides because they wanted to share the data with their teams. One person — David, who's usually very direct — said "clear and concise, thanks Sam." Nobody said anything negative. Nobody gave me a weird look. Nobody seemed to notice the trembling or the number slip.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Let's go back to your behavioral experiment worksheet now. Pull that out if you have it. Your prediction was — remind me the exact wording.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [reading] "I will go blank in the middle of the presentation, stumble badly and lose my place, and people will think I'm incompetent and wonder why I was even hired. Belief level: 70%." And my alternative prediction was: "I will be nervous but get through it. People will focus on the content, not my delivery. Belief level: 20%."

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And looking at the actual outcome — which prediction did reality match more closely?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: The alternative one. Clearly. I was nervous but I got through it. People focused on the data. Nobody questioned my competence. If anything, I got positive feedback.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: So the evidence directly contradicted your catastrophic prediction. Let me ask the next column on your worksheet — what's your belief now in the catastrophic prediction? "I will go blank and be judged as incompetent."

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [pause] Honestly? Still like 40%. I know rationally that it went well this time. But part of me thinks I just got lucky. Or that this was a small, friendly audience and it wouldn't go the same way in a bigger or more critical setting.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Okay, so the belief dropped from 70% to 40%. That's significant movement. But I want to examine the thoughts keeping it at 40%. You said two things: "I got lucky" and "this was a special case." Those are what we call discounting strategies — cognitive maneuvers your anxiety uses to explain away contradictory evidence so it can maintain its hold. Let's look at "I got lucky." What does luck mean in this context?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Like... the stars aligned. My preparation was perfect enough to cover for my incompetence. If I hadn't rehearsed fifteen times, the real me would have shown up — nervous, bumbling, forgettable.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Interesting. So your brain is telling you that preparation is somehow cheating? That the "real" you is nervous and bumbling, and competent performance is an illusion you manufactured?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [pause] Yeah. When you say it back like that, it sounds... not rational.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: What's an alternative way to interpret the fact that you prepared thoroughly and then performed well?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: That... preparation is a skill? That preparing well and delivering well is just... what competent people do?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Exactly. Every good presenter prepares. Steve Jobs rehearsed his keynotes hundreds of times. That wasn't luck or cheating — that was professionalism. Your preparation is evidence of competence, not evidence of faking. The thought "I just got lucky" is what we call a cognitive distortion — specifically, discounting the positive. It dismisses your success by attributing it to external factors rather than internal skill. What about the "special case" thought?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: That one says: "This was twelve friendly colleagues who already know and like me. Of course they were nice. A real audience — strangers, senior leaders — would be different."

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: So the goalposts have moved. Before the experiment, your fear was "I'll fail in front of my team." You didn't fail. Now the fear says "okay, but I'll fail in front of HARDER audiences." This is what anxiety does — it retreats to the next fortified position. It never says "okay, you won, I'll leave now." It shifts the fear forward. The only way to chase it out of each position is to run more experiments in progressively challenging settings. Which brings me to the next question: what's the next behavioral experiment?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I've been thinking about this a lot actually. There's a cross-team presentation opportunity next month — the mid-year review. Twenty people, ten-minute slot. Bigger audience, higher stakes, some people I don't know well. My manager asked if anyone wanted to volunteer and I haven't responded yet.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That sounds like an excellent target experiment, but it's a big jump from twelve known colleagues to twenty mixed audience in one step. In our exposure hierarchy from session three, where does that fall?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Probably a 7 out of 10 on the fear hierarchy. The team meeting was maybe a 6.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: So we're only stepping up one rung. That might be okay if we build in intermediate steps between now and then. What's something between a 5 and a 7 that you could do this week or next?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Hmm. A couple things come to mind. One — asking a question in our department sync meeting. That's about twenty people, I wouldn't be presenting but I'd be speaking up voluntarily. That terrifies me because right now I keep my camera off and stay completely silent in those meetings.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: What's your prediction if you speak up in the sync?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: That my voice will shake and people will think "why is she asking that, it's a stupid question" and there'll be an awkward silence.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Rate that belief.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Maybe 55%.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And the alternative?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: That people will either answer normally or say "good question" and move on. Nobody will care as much as I think they will.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Rate that.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good. Now, there are actually two exposure elements here: turning your camera on, and speaking. Those are separate fears, right?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah. Even camera-on makes me anxious. Because then I'm visible. I watch my own face in the little box and I monitor my expressions — "do I look weird? Am I making the right face? What if I yawn and someone sees?"

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: The self-monitoring while on camera — that's the same mechanism as when you were watching your hands shake during the presentation. Self-focused attention. It generates anxiety because you're processing yourself as an object being observed rather than a participant in the meeting. Do you want to split this into two steps? Camera-on this week, speaking up next week?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Camera on I can do this week. The speaking... [deep breath] ...you know what, let me try both. Camera on AND one question. If I'm going to be uncomfortable, might as well go all in.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: I love that spirit, but let me check — is that courage talking or is that the "might as well jump off the cliff" urgency that sometimes leads to avoidance afterward when it feels like too much?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [laughs] Fair question. It's courage. I think. The team meeting went well enough that I have a tiny bit of momentum and I want to use it before the anxiety erodes it.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Okay. Then let's plan it. Camera on plus one question in the department sync. When is that meeting?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Thursday at 2 PM.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good. Let's do some in-session preparation. What question could you ask? Is there a topic that typically comes up where you have a genuine question?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: The engineering team always gives an update on the product roadmap. I usually have questions about timelines but I never ask — I just Slack the person afterward privately. I could ask during the meeting instead.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Perfect. Can you draft a question now? Something specific enough that you've got it ready.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Um... "Can you give us a sense of the timeline for the payment feature rollout? My team is planning marketing materials around it and we want to make sure we're aligned."

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's a clear, professional, purposeful question. There's no universe in which anyone thinks that's stupid. You're asking for information your team needs. Write that down on a sticky note and have it in front of you during the meeting. If you lose your nerve in the moment, you have the exact words already.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Like a script. Okay. That helps. The scripting helped with the presentation too.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: It's a scaffold. Over time, as your confidence builds, you'll need less scripting. But for now, removing the "what do I say?" uncertainty reduces the cognitive load, which leaves more bandwidth for managing the anxiety. Now — your prediction for Thursday. Write it out: "If I turn my camera on and ask this question, what do I think will happen?"

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [writing] "My voice will shake. People will look at me funny. There will be an awkward pause before someone answers. Belief: 50%."

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Alternative prediction?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: "Someone will answer normally, probably with a timeline estimate. Nobody will focus on my delivery. Belief: 40%."

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Notice your alternative is getting stronger — 40% versus 20% before the last experiment. Your brain is slowly accumulating evidence for the non-catastrophic outcome. That's the process working. Good. Now let's shift to something else you mentioned last time — the Friday lunch group situation. Tell me what happened with that.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Oh! So my team does these informal Friday lunches. Everyone goes to this taco place down the street. I've been declining for months — saying I have calls, or I brought food, or I have a deadline. All lies. I was just too anxious to sit at a table with five people making conversation for forty-five minutes.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And this Friday?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I said yes. [pause] I almost backed out at 11:45 when everyone was getting their jackets. My heart was pounding. But I thought about our work on avoidance and I just... went.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: What was the experience like?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Awkward at first. Like the first ten minutes I didn't know what to do with my face or my hands. I was hyper-aware of myself — "am I eating too slowly? Too fast? Am I chewing weird? Is there something on my face?" Classic self-monitoring. But then someone asked about weekend plans and I mentioned I was thinking of trying a new hiking trail and three people got excited and started giving recommendations and suddenly I was IN the conversation without trying.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: What happened to the self-monitoring at that point?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: It disappeared. Like a switch flipped. I stopped watching myself and started watching them. Listening to their stories. Asking follow-up questions. And honestly? They're nice people. Funny. I've worked with them for two years and didn't know that Emma has a golden retriever puppy or that David does stand-up comedy on weekends.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: So when your attention shifted from self-focused to other-focused, the anxiety dropped and the connection increased. That's one of the core mechanisms we target in social anxiety treatment. Self-focused attention is the engine that drives the anxiety. It generates hyper-awareness of imperfections that other people don't notice. When your attention moves outward — to the conversation, the food, the other person's face — the anxiety loses its fuel source.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: It's like I can only be anxious OR engaged, not both at the same time. Is that normal?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's exactly right. Attention is finite. If it's directed outward, there's less available for the internal monitoring loop. That's actually a technique we can formalize — deliberate attention shifting. When you notice yourself monitoring, consciously redirect: "What is this person saying? What color is their shirt? What's on the menu?" It breaks the self-focus loop.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I accidentally did that. By asking questions. "Oh, you hike? Where do you go?" The questions forced me to look outward.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Asking questions is actually a brilliant social anxiety strategy because it does three things simultaneously: it shifts your attention to the other person, it gives you a role in the conversation that feels low-risk (listener, not performer), and it makes the other person feel good because people love talking about themselves.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [laughs] That's my whole strategy — just ask questions and let other people talk.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And that's completely valid! The belief that you need to be entertaining, witty, or perform in social settings is a cognitive distortion. Most positive social interactions are about presence and curiosity, not performance. People feel connected when they feel heard. You don't need to be the funniest person at the table — you need to be genuinely interested.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Okay. That reframe helps. I've always thought I'm boring — like, why would anyone want to talk to ME? I don't have interesting stories.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: There's the "I'm boring" core belief. Let's test that. At the lunch, did anyone seem bored by you? Did anyone look away, check their phone, try to end the conversation?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: No. Actually one person — Emma — said "glad you could make it this time!" in this genuinely warm way. Like she'd noticed I'd been absent and was happy I was there.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: So the social environment is actually welcoming. People want you there. Your brain has been telling you the environment is hostile, judging, critical — but the evidence says otherwise. That's the fundamental misappraisal in social anxiety: your threat detection system is mislabeling safe situations as dangerous. Each time you approach instead of avoid, and discover safety instead of danger, you're recalibrating that system.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: How many times do I have to approach before my brain gets the message?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good question. Research suggests that consistent exposure over 12-20 repetitions typically produces significant belief change. You've done one team presentation and one lunch. We need more data points. Your brain is like a skeptical scientist — one study isn't enough. It needs replication. Which is why this week's plan is important: camera on in the sync, ask a question, and accept the Friday lunch invitation again. Three exposures in one week.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Three. Okay. That feels doable with the momentum.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Let me also introduce something for next session. I want us to start working on the "I'm boring" core belief more directly. Between now and then, I want you to notice moments when people respond positively to you — not neutral, but warm. Smiles, invitations, compliments, any indication that someone is choosing to engage with you. Write them down. We're collecting evidence against "I'm boring."

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Like a positive data log?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Exactly. A positive data log. Your brain has a negativity filter — it deletes positive social data and amplifies negative. We're going to force it to retain the positive by writing it down. Even small things — a colleague saying "good morning" warmly, someone laughing at something you said, someone seeking you out for conversation.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Okay. I can do that. I might have to look hard for it though.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And the act of looking — that attention direction — will itself change what you notice. When you're scanning for positives instead of negatives, your experience shifts. Alright, let's also touch briefly on something from last session. You mentioned that your social anxiety affects dating too. Any movement there?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [exhale] No. That's still... the scariest thing on the hierarchy. Like, a 10. I haven't even opened the dating app in three weeks.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's okay. We're not there yet. We're building confidence in professional settings first, and the skills transfer. The attention-shifting, the behavioral experiments, the cognitive restructuring — all of that applies to dating too. When you're ready, we'll build an exposure hierarchy specifically for that domain. But not today.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Thank god. [laughs] One terrifying thing at a time.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Exactly. Graduated exposure. Not flooding. You're doing beautifully, Samantha. Let me summarize this week's plan: Thursday department sync — camera on, ask the prepared question about the payment feature timeline. Friday lunch — accept the invitation, practice attention-shifting by asking questions and noticing others. Daily positive data log — write down one positive social interaction per day. All of that sound manageable?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yes. It's a lot but it's all things I now know I CAN do. That presentation proved something to me. I didn't die. The world didn't end. My career didn't implode. I just... did it.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And that "I just did it" — that's the beginning of a new self-narrative. You're not "the person who can't speak up." You're "the person who gets nervous and does it anyway." Those are very different identities.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [quiet] Yeah. They really are. I want to be the second one.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: You already are. You just haven't fully updated the story yet. The evidence is building. One experiment at a time. Any questions before we wrap?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Actually, one thing. The cross-team presentation next month — should I volunteer? Part of me wants to before I chicken out. But another part says that's maybe too aggressive.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Here's what I'd suggest: reply to your manager's email and express interest. Say something like "I'd like to be considered for a slot. Can I confirm by next week?" That gives you a commitment without full commitment. We'll prepare together over the next few sessions. And if when the time comes your anxiety is still too high, we can use it as a planning exercise rather than an action one.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: "I'd like to be considered." Okay. That I can send. Today even. Before my anxiety talks me out of it.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Do it right after our session while the courage is fresh. Remember — courage is not the absence of fear. It's action in the presence of fear. You've been courageous every single week since we started this work.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: It's weird — the more I do these things, the less power the anxiety has. But I also feel like I'll never be fully comfortable. Like, will I always have this before presentations?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Full comfort may not be the goal — and honestly may not be desirable. Some nervousness before public speaking is normal and even enhancing. A little adrenaline sharpens focus and energy. It's only a problem when it stops you from living the life you want. The goal isn't zero anxiety. The goal is functional comfort — being able to do the things that matter to you despite some anxiety being present.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Functional comfort. I like that frame a lot. It takes the pressure off of "curing" myself and makes it about living.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Exactly. You don't need to be cured. You need to be free. And freedom doesn't mean the absence of fear — it means the presence of choice despite fear. You're choosing more and more. I'm really proud of the work you're doing.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Thank you, Dr. Torres. Really. I feel like a different person than six weeks ago. Or maybe the same person, just... braver.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Same person, new evidence, new behaviors, new story. I'll see you next Tuesday. Send that email today!

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Sending it now. [holds up phone] Before I leave this office.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: [laughs] That's behavioral activation in real time. Go get 'em.

goals: Behavioral experiments for fear of public speaking at work
